sentences.text: My name is-- now?
Sonia Sklaver, S-O-N-I-A.
Sklaver, S-K-L-A-V-E-R.
My name at birth was Shaindel Kritczmar, S-H-A-I-N-D-E-L.
Kritczmar, K-R-I-T-C-Z-M-A-R.
January 5, 1925.
I was born in the town of Hoszcza.
At that time, 1925, it was Poland.
Hoszcza has many spelling.
It has-- in the Polish spelling, it's H-O-S-Z-C-Z-A, Hoszcza.
They used to say that it had lots of mud.
In Hoszcza, there were very thick mud.
The word Hoszcza means [NON-ENGLISH], which is apparently what they had in mind.
We-- we call it Hoscht, H-O-S-C-H-T, Hoscht.
Poland.
Many times.
Poland was many times in-- occupied by the Soviet-- not the Soviet-- the czarist Russia and Austria.
Austria.
And Poland was established as an independent state in 1920.
Between 1918 and 1920, it was a Polish state.
It lasted until 1939 until the Second World War broke out.
Mordechai in Hebrew.
Mordko, they called him in Polish.
And Mordko, M-O-R-D-K-O.
Mordechai, M-O-R-D-C-H-I-- A-I.
Kritczmar.
Hinda, H-I-N-D-A.
It was Goldbitter.
Gold, G-O-L-D.
And bitter, B-I-T-T-E-R.
Yes, we were at four children-- a brother, Abraham.
[NON-ENGLISH], he was called for short and born 1911.
My sister was 1913.
Her name was Perel.
[NON-ENGLISH], called by the Polish people.
And [NON-ENGLISH] remained mainly in-- in her life now even.
And Leah was [NON-ENGLISH] sometimes called or Leah.
And I was the youngest.
My brother left in 1932 as a [NON-ENGLISH] with a young bride to Israel.
And he, of course, survived.
At time, it was Palestine called.
It became an independent state in 1948.
And he has two sons.
My sister left for Russia just as the Soviets were-- were, you know, fleeing when Germany attacked them.
That was 1941-- in the spring of 1941.
And she survived.
Her husband was in the army all through the war.
And he-- they didn't find each other until after the war.
He was under Stalingrad.
The Russian Army.
Stalingrad, he was.
He was wounded in the eye-- seriously wounded.
And my sister Leah, she was with, you know, almost until the end.
She survived.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] actually, she didn't survive.
So--
It was close when the-- when the Germans were fleeing.
Apparently, there was a fire in the village.
And when she was discovered, they reported her to the authorities.
I had the regards from people that worked.
They were shoe makers.
And they-- very highly regarded in the villages, because they were sewing shoes and boots for the farmers.
They were from the next town to ours, Tuchyn.
And they survived.
They saw her.
They did not know that she was hiding.
They maybe suspected, but they didn't know she was hiding until that fire that happened.
The fire was close to the liberation [INAUDIBLE].
In the winter of '45.
It was-- at first, we had a very small, little house, not too big, but enough for us.
And the ceiling was always-- always like a-- a stomach kind I had.
[LAUGHS] And I was afraid it going to fall some day.
But finally, we had a very beautiful home built between 1930 and 1932.
A new, beautiful home.
We had a tenant.
And they were always the military people, the tenants.
In other words, we had a military garrison stationed in Hoszcza.
We were on the border.
And that's why the military was there.
And that was the Polish military I'm talking about.
They [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they had-- the, you know, the-- the couple would leave there.
But the husband would go to the military.
There was a large park with concerns or something like that with building.
The park was off-limits to the Jewish people.
Only on the days of the 1st of May-- not the 1st, the 3rd of May.
That's when the-- the Polish people celebrated independence.
Always off-limits.
Always off-limits.
And I wouldn't say maybe not just to the Polish people-- to the Jewish people.
To the Polish people just as-- to the Ukrainian, I would say.
All civilians were off-limits and Jews, of course.
There was a-- a large gate.
And it was a-- gated all over.
One gate was, I remember, on farther part of the town, which was more on the outskirts.
And one was in the town that I will show you the picture later.
It was a very beautiful place with a backyard and lots of greens and trees and a small little brook running.
And my oldest brother-- my older brother, he was always out of the house.
He was in school.
He was in the town of Rowne.
He was then at gymnasium.
And my sister, too, was in another town.
And she was in Ostrog and studying, also gymnasium.
My sister Leah and I were always at home with-- there is this-- my mother would prepare the goods, the challah, and the good kefirs to bake.
Early, early in the morning Friday, she would have the oven lit and bake the good stuff.
And we would tasted a very fresh [NON-ENGLISH], which was something like a [PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS]
No, flatbread something.
It was wood.
Wood, right.
Big oven.
Except that, of course, the heating was also done in special ovens.
Everything with wood.
Most of the time, wood, yeah.
He was a-- we had a store, a yard good business, retail.
My mother was with him in the store.
And I would help out also sometimes.
Run in after school to get some money for ice cream or soda.
Right.
They were the Ukrainians and very few Polish people and of course, the Jewish population.
Our town was-- was in the middle and surrounded by-- by Ukrainians, villagers.
The town had a mall, I would say-- something like a modern mall-- with stores on each side of the-- of the long, long building, all under one roof.
Across from the stores was a pharmacy, my uncle's pharmacy.
Right across from the pharmacy--
--in the mall.
Next to him was his-- my uncle's-- his brother's store.
And a little farther was my husband's, Irving's, store-- hardware store.
Population was 1,000 Jewish people.
And--
In town, only in the park.
Those were the military and their families.
Mainly their families lived on the outside of the park.
And that's how we had a tenant, which was [INAUDIBLE] but had a beautiful, large dog, Dalmatian.
And he was my-- I was his favorite, and he was mine.
None of the Jewish children had dogs.
But we had lots of cats.
Cats for the mice to protect.
And I had many, many cats.
The mother, the father, the children.
They called me [NON-ENGLISH], which means the Mother of the Cat.
On Friday evening, my father-- we would have dinner early, Shabbos meal.
And he would put his warm, warm [INAUDIBLE] on during the winter.
[NON-ENGLISH] is a coat that had the fur inside.
And on the outside was just a wool cloth.
And he would walk to shul.
The shul was-- everything was within walking distance in our town.
And he would study missionaries, [INAUDIBLE] with them.
Come very late home.
And in the morning, he would go back to shul in the morning-- Shabbos morning.
I do not remember that my mother went to shul on Shabbos.
But on the holidays, of course.
The holidays were wonderful, really wonderful.
Starting from the spring, Purim.
Purim was-- was especially interesting, because the out of town young people would come and perform for-- for our town.
That was probably for food stock and for-- for charity.
But at first, I would be afraid when I was very young, you know.
They would come in in outfits and costumes.
And later, I loved it, because they sang and they performed and they would leave, you know.
They would be fed first, and then they would leave.
Then Passover was such an ordeal of preparing.
Yes.
The-- the Passover stuff was stored, part of fit, in the buffet in the living room, dining room area.
And the rest, pots and pans, in the attic.
And everything would come down and be washed and [NON-ENGLISH].
Matzo was-- was prepared in-- in special places of [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] matzo baking.
It was baked by hand at that time.
Town.
Town, yeah.
It was large ovens.
And I would run in to see and taste a piece of matzo.
Sometimes a lot of children from the school would also go and participate and help in matzo baking.
And we had a very nice Seders.
Two hour Seder would come my aunt from Dombrovitz.
She and her husband and the two children and [?
Gania. ?] And [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Uncle [INAUDIBLE], [CRYING] his two children, [INAUDIBLE].
[INAUDIBLE] is a survivor.
She survived in Russia, and she's now in Washington DC.
Yosef, her brother, is somewhere in Russia.
We-- we don't know where.
We're not sure.
Not sure.
We have never heard from him, although he knows where we are.
And he knows that he has a family in Washington DC.
And this Seder was conducted until late.
I would always fall asleep.
Always fall asleep in the chair.
For the-- for the holidays would come my brother [NON-ENGLISH], as long as he was still in Poland, and my sister Perel from gymnasium.
They both arrived.
So the family celebrated.
And the very first thing after Passover, I couldn't fast enough run to the baker to get a piece of bread.
Passover was not a good time [LAUGHTER] for my diet.
Stayed, of course.
The town was, I would say, quite a distance from us.
And that was the name Dombrovitz.
It was not Ukraine.
It was Policja, which is north from Ukraine.
They would come by that time, I think, a train up to Rowne, and take buses to the town of Hoszcza.
And Shabbos was exceptionally beautiful, because we were near a very large river, [NON-ENGLISH].
Around the river, there were pastures.
And at times, it was covered all with-- with white flowers, daisies.
And then it was the buttercups in yellow.
And we would get lots of flowers to the house.
Some homes, I remember, in Hoszcza did not have any wooden floors yet.
They had dirt that every-- before Shabbos they would spray a certain mixture and freshly, like, paint it.
But we had wooden floors already.
As a matter of fact, we even had running water.
In the very first-- in the old house, we even had a radio, which was with-- with--
Headphones.
At times, I would fall asleep and schlep the whole thing with me.
My brother was, you know, instrumental in getting that.
And at that time, I remember also the electricity in town.
No telephones, just those new inventions.
Electricity, yes, even in the old house and in the new house, of course.
It was only running water.
Running water.
No.
That was down in the basement.
Yeah.
Yeah.
But there was a laundry room with a-- a zinc metal bathtub.
Laundry was done not too often.
There was always a lady that would come and help with the laundry.
And the [INAUDIBLE]-- and in the-- I would say like every season, a seamstress would come to sew linen.
I don't think you could have bought them in any other store.
So she would, you know, sew them to-- to each, you know, whatever-- whatever could be fixed, she would repair.
And what not, make new pillowcases, new-- new sheets, new quilt covers.
Not this one.
Another one.
Another one.
She was an old lady, lived out of town.
But she loved to sew cute little dresses for the-- for the townspeople.
Yeah.
School-- I started with a public school.
Very few Jewish people-- Jewish children were in the public school.
They had-- they had mainly Ukrainian and a few Polish children.
The school-- everything was within walking distance.
And I did not stay there more than until three to four grade, because all my friends started to attend the [NON-ENGLISH], the Hebrew school in-- that was established in our town.
There was one already 1918, which my sister and brother probably attended.
But the last one was established in the-- in the early '30s.
It was Tarbut, which means education.
Public school.
It was Polish and Ukrainian was taught.
And I had private-- private lessons at home of Hebrew.

But then I did not have to have the private lessons at home, because Tarbut school had-- had everything in Hebrew, except the Polish language and Polish history, because this is to be accredited as a-- by the government, the Polish government.
That's why we had to have Polish language as a language and-- and Polish history also in Polish.
All other subjects were in Hebrew.
I was friendly there with the Christian children, Ukrainian and Polish.
I visited their homes, and they visited our home.
So though they were-- lived on the outskirts of the town-- the town.
And I was a little bit uncomfortable there, because especially my name being Shaindel, they made fun of me.
And I-- I did have Jewish friends.
The Jewish girl friends lived very close.
I wouldn't say an experience that I could remember.
But I know that they made fun of the Jewish children.
Yes.
I was never harmed.
And I was in very-- I was in very good relations with the non-Jewish children.
The-- and it showed up later on during the Holocaust.
No one but no one wanted to-- to take-- to hide or to save Jews.
The relationship between the Jews and the Gentiles.
Yeah.
Before-- before the war.
They had very-- they-- they would come to town to shop.
In our store-- in my parents' store, they had credit.
And when the harvest would be collected and sold, then they had the money to pay.
They--
Mostly.
Mostly Ukrainians.
Mostly Ukrainians, yes.
